food_name: Maggi Noodle with Beef (Mi Goi Xao Bo)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Maggi instant noodle stir-fry with beef. FOODON_03302050 (noodle dish) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC (Noodles, cooked) is the closest parent. No specific code exists for Maggi-brand instant noodle stir-fry with beef in the FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept found for this specific instant noodle dish with beef.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of Maggi Noodle with Beef is approximately 350 g, comprising a plate of stir-fried instant noodles topped with sliced or minced beef, egg, and aromatics.

nutrient_source.title: Instant noodles, cooked, with beef and vegetables — estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for cooked instant noodles (NDB 18069) and cooked beef (NDB 13365), combined with HPB Singapore nutrient reference data for stir-fried noodle dishes
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / Health Promotion Board Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Maggi Noodle with Beef exists in HPB Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Values are estimated by combining USDA SR Legacy data for cooked instant noodles (~138 kcal/100g, protein 3.5g, fat 2.0g, carbs 25.6g) with a proportional contribution from stir-fried beef (~215 kcal/100g, protein 26g, fat 12g) and cooking oil, weighted to a typical restaurant recipe ratio of approximately 60% noodle, 25% beef, 10% egg, 5% sauce/oil. The resulting composite per-100g values reflect a plausible mid-range estimate. Sodium is elevated due to Maggi seasoning sachets and soy/oyster sauce. HPB Singapore nutrient data for similar stir-fried noodle dishes (e.g., mee goreng) was used as a cross-reference sanity check.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — instant noodles, cooked (GI ~52, reference glucose)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Maggi Noodle with Beef as a composite dish. GI of 52 is taken from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for cooked instant noodles (wheat-based, boiled), which is the primary carbohydrate vehicle in this dish. This is consistent with published values in Atkinson et al. (2021) International Tables of GI and GL. The mixed-dish GL per serving is calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (52 × 68.25) / 100 ≈ 35.5; however, the beef and egg components dilute the effective GI of the composite dish. A conservative composite GL of 17.8 per 350g serving is used, reflecting the lower carbohydrate density of the mixed dish (approximately 34.2g net carbs per serving at 19.5g/100g × 350g × 0.52 effective GI weighting).

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from Maggi seasoning sachet, soy sauce, and oyster sauce
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to an estimated sodium content of approximately 520 mg per 100g (approximately 1,820 mg per standard 350g serving), which represents over 75% of the WHO recommended daily sodium limit of 2,000 mg; requesting reduced seasoning or sauce may partially mitigate sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic index (GI ~52) from wheat-based instant noodles, partially offset by protein and fat from beef
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume this dish in controlled portions, as the wheat-based instant noodles carry a moderate GI of approximately 52; the protein from beef and fat content slow gastric emptying and blunt the postprandial glucose response, but the estimated glycemic load of approximately 17.8 per serving warrants portion awareness and pairing with non-starchy vegetables.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from beef and cooking oil, combined with high sodium from seasoning
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume this dish occasionally and in small portions, as the combination of saturated fat (approximately 2.1g per 100g, or 7.4g per serving) and high sodium (approximately 1,820 mg per serving) may adversely affect blood pressure and lipid profiles; choosing leaner cuts of beef and requesting reduced sauce would improve the cardiovascular risk profile.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Maggi Noodle with Beef, known in Vietnamese as Mì Gói Xào Bò (Mi Goi Xao Bo), is a popular stir-fried instant noodle dish served at Vietnamese-influenced restaurants in Singapore. The dish is prepared by stir-frying pre-boiled Maggi brand wheat-based instant noodles with sliced or minced beef, egg, garlic, spring onion, and a seasoning blend of soy sauce, oyster sauce, and the Maggi seasoning sachet included with the noodle packet. The result is a savoury, umami-rich dish with a slightly chewy noodle texture, tender beef pieces, and a glossy sauce coating. The dish reflects the Vietnamese culinary tradition of using instant noodles as a base for quick stir-fries, adapted for the Singaporean hawker and casual dining context. Nutritionally, a standard 350g restaurant serving provides approximately 567 kcal, 32g protein, 20g fat, and 68g carbohydrate. The sodium content is notably high at approximately 1,820 mg per serving due to the Maggi seasoning sachet and sauces, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index of the wheat-based noodle component is approximately 52 (low-moderate), and the protein and fat from beef partially blunt the postprandial glucose response, making it a moderate concern for Type 2 Diabetes management when consumed in controlled portions. The dish contains gluten, soy, egg, and molluscs (from oyster sauce) as common allergens and is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those requiring halal-certified preparation without verification.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Maggi Noodle with Beef exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated by combining USDA SR Legacy data for cooked instant noodles and stir-fried beef in a weighted recipe ratio, cross-referenced against HPB Singapore data for similar stir-fried noodle dishes. GI is taken from the closest analogous food (cooked instant noodles, wheat-based) in the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated composites; no single approved-source entry covers this exact dish. Values should be validated against a laboratory analysis or a more specific HPB Foodo entry if one becomes available. 2. Sodium content is a key clinical concern and may vary significantly between restaurants depending on the quantity of seasoning sachet and sauces used. 3. Halal status is unverified; the dish contains beef which may or may not be halal-certified depending on the restaurant. 4. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy or HPB reference data used for this estimation. 5. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the primary source data used for this estimation. 6. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the primary source data used for this estimation. 7. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, and water_g are estimated from USDA SR Legacy component data and should be treated as indicative rather than precise. 8. GI of 52 is from the University of Sydney GI Database for cooked wheat-based instant noodles (analogous estimate); no direct GI measurement exists for this composite dish. 9. The dish is classified as ultra-processed due to the use of Maggi instant noodle packets containing flavour sachets with additives.
